refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall maintain teleoperation communication packet loss at or below the customer-defined threshold (acceptance criterion: ≤1% under agreed network conditions) to ensure safe and effective remote equipment operation.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-009 addresses a critical quality-of-service requirement for teleoperation. Packet loss directly impacts operator situational awareness, control responsiveness, and safety. Refining the FR to explicitly reference the acceptance criterion (≤1%) and network conditions clarifies the measurable boundary and reduces ambiguity in verification.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Conduct network stress testing under agreed worksite conditions (bandwidth, latency, interference profiles) with teleoperation commands and telemetry streams active. Measure packet loss rate over representative mission duration. Verify that loss remains ≤1% and that operator can safely command equipment without perceptible lag or command loss.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-009.1: The teleoperation subsystem shall transmit operator commands with ≤1% packet loss under agreed network conditions.
FR-NGHE-009.2: The teleoperation subsystem shall receive vehicle telemetry with ≤1% packet loss under agreed network conditions.
FR-NGHE-009.3: The teleoperation subsystem shall detect and report packet loss events to the Safety Services module for operator awareness and mission abort decision support.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Implement redundant communication channels (dual network paths) with automatic failover to maintain packet loss below threshold.
DP-B: Deploy adaptive bitrate encoding and forward error correction (FEC) to recover lost packets and reduce effective packet loss impact.
DP-C: Implement network quality monitoring with dynamic command throttling and priority queuing to protect critical safety messages.
DP-D: Combine redundant channels (DP-A) with FEC (DP-B) and quality monitoring (DP-C) to achieve defense-in-depth packet loss mitigation.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-D: Implement a defense-in-depth packet loss mitigation strategy combining (1) redundant communication channels with automatic failover, (2) forward error correction and adaptive encoding, and (3) real-time network quality monitoring with priority queuing for safety-critical messages.

potential_sources: DP-D couples teleoperation control logic (command transmission) with network infrastructure (redundancy, FEC, monitoring). This is acceptable because packet loss is fundamentally a network-layer concern that must be addressed at the communication boundary.

DP-D couples safety alerting (quality monitoring) with teleoperation (priority queuing). This is acceptable and necessary: safety criticality demands that loss-of-communication events trigger Safety Services alerts.
coupling_check.notes: The coupling is intentional and justified by safety criticality. No hidden dependencies on unrelated FRs detected. The design is modular: each sub-strategy (redundancy, FEC, monitoring) can be independently validated and toggled based on network conditions.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Customer-agreed network conditions are well-defined and stable (e.g., 4G LTE, WiFi 6, or hybrid). If conditions vary significantly, packet loss targets may be unachievable.
ASSUMPTION: Redundant channels (e.g., dual cellular or satellite backup) are available and economically feasible at the worksite.
RISK: FEC adds latency and computational overhead. If latency budget is tight, FEC may degrade operator responsiveness despite reducing packet loss.
RISK: Priority queuing may starve non-critical telemetry (e.g., equipment diagnostics) during congestion, reducing operator situational awareness.
RISK: Automatic failover logic must be robust; incorrect failover decisions could cause command loss or duplication.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP-D to explicitly specify: (1) failover decision criteria and latency tolerance, (2) FEC overhead budget and latency impact, (3) priority queue rules (which messages are critical vs. deferrable), and (4) monitoring thresholds that trigger Safety Services alerts. This will reduce ambiguity in implementation and testing.

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: The Teleoperation module owns the end-to-end communication pipeline for remote equipment operation, including command transmission, telemetry reception, network quality monitoring, and packet loss mitigation logic (redundancy, FEC, priority queuing). The module interfaces with the Vehicle (for command/telemetry endpoints) and Safety Services (for loss-of-communication alerts).

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Operator commands (movement, tool actuation, mode changes) flow from the tele-operator console into the Teleoperation module via the Tasking interface. This is the primary input for command transmission.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Vehicle state, sensor readings, and equipment status flow from the Vehicle module to the Teleoperation module via Telemetry. The Teleoperation module processes, buffers, and prioritizes telemetry for transmission to the tele-operator console.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: The Teleoperation module uses the Network Link interface to transmit commands and receive telemetry over the agreed network infrastructure (cellular, WiFi, satellite, or hybrid). Network quality metrics (latency, jitter, packet loss) are monitored via this interface.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: When packet loss exceeds threshold or communication is lost, the Teleoperation module sends SafetyAlert messages to the Safety Services module to trigger operator warnings, mission abort procedures, or autonomous safety actions.

module_containment_scores.notes: NGHE Solution is the top-level system container. Assigning packet loss mitigation here would diffuse responsibility across all subsystems and obscure ownership. Not recommended.

Vehicle module owns the equipment hardware and local control logic. Packet loss mitigation is a communication-layer concern, not a vehicle-layer concern. Assigning here would couple vehicle firmware to network infrastructure, reducing modularity.

Teleoperation module is the natural owner of end-to-end communication quality. It sits at the boundary between operator commands and vehicle telemetry, making it the ideal location for redundancy, FEC, and quality monitoring. High cohesion, minimal diffusion.

Safety Services owns safety alerting and mission abort logic, not communication infrastructure. While SafetyAlert messages originate from Teleoperation, the Safety Services module should not own the packet loss mitigation mechanisms themselves.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The Teleoperation module is the optimal owner of packet loss mitigation because: (1) it owns the communication pipeline (Tasking in, Telemetry out, Network Link inout), (2) it has direct visibility into network quality metrics, (3) it can implement redundancy, FEC, and priority queuing without coupling to vehicle hardware or safety logic, and (4) it maintains a clear interface contract with Safety Services (SafetyAlert out) for loss-of-communication events.
containment_risks: RISK: If the Teleoperation module becomes too large (redundancy logic + FEC + priority queuing + monitoring), it may need internal decomposition into sub-modules (e.g., NetworkQualityMonitor, PacketRecovery, CommandPrioritizer). This is acceptable and expected at the next decomposition level.

RISK: The Teleoperation module must handle both command and telemetry streams with different packet loss sensitivities. Commands are safety-critical; telemetry is informational. The module must implement asymmetric protection strategies, which adds complexity.

RISK: If network conditions change dynamically (e.g., operator moves between 4G and WiFi), the Teleoperation module must adapt its mitigation strategy in real-time. This requires robust state management and failover logic.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Confirm that the Teleoperation module's scope includes network quality monitoring and failover logic. If the module is currently limited to command/telemetry routing, expand its charter to include packet loss mitigation. Additionally, define clear interfaces for network condition reporting (e.g., a NetworkQuality interface) to enable Safety Services and the operator console to react to degraded conditions.

fr_dp_gate.reason: The selected DP (defense-in-depth packet loss mitigation) directly addresses the FR (maintain ≤1% packet loss). The DP is implementable, testable, and justified by safety criticality. No logical gaps or contradictions detected.

diffusion_risks: The Teleoperation module will own three distinct sub-strategies (redundancy, FEC, monitoring). At the next decomposition level, these should be clearly separated into sub-modules or components to avoid monolithic design.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: The Teleoperation module is the clear owner. It has high containment (0.95) and low diffusion (0.05). All external interfaces (Tasking, Telemetry, Network Link, SafetyAlert) are well-defined and align with the module's scope. No orphaned responsibilities detected.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Verification is feasible via network stress testing under agreed conditions. Packet loss can be measured directly, and operator responsiveness can be assessed qualitatively. Acceptance criterion (≤1%) is clear and measurable.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: The allocation of packet loss mitigation to the Teleoperation module is clear. The module owns the communication pipeline and has direct control over redundancy, FEC, and monitoring. No ambiguity in responsibility.

recommended_revisions: Refine the DP to specify failover decision criteria, FEC latency budget, and priority queue rules before detailed design.
Define a NetworkQuality interface or telemetry stream to expose network metrics to Safety Services and the operator console.
At the next decomposition level, decompose the Teleoperation module into sub-modules for redundancy management, FEC/encoding, and quality monitoring to avoid monolithic design.

decision.justification: The FR is well-refined, the DP is sound and justified by safety criticality, and the IM assignment is clear (Teleoperation module with high containment). However, the DP is not fully atomic: it combines three distinct strategies (redundancy, FEC, monitoring) that will require further decomposition. Proceeding to the next level will clarify implementation details, reduce ambiguity, and enable detailed design and testing.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-009.1.1: The Teleoperation module shall implement redundant communication channels (primary and backup) with automatic failover to maintain command transmission availability.
FR-NGHE-009.1.2: The Teleoperation module shall implement forward error correction (FEC) and adaptive bitrate encoding to recover lost packets and reduce effective packet loss impact.
FR-NGHE-009.1.3: The Teleoperation module shall monitor network quality metrics (latency, jitter, packet loss rate) in real-time and adjust mitigation strategies dynamically.
FR-NGHE-009.1.4: The Teleoperation module shall implement priority queuing to protect safety-critical messages (commands, SafetyAlert) from being dropped during congestion.

assumptions: Customer-agreed network conditions are well-defined and stable (e.g., 4G LTE, WiFi 6, or hybrid).
Redundant communication channels (dual cellular, satellite backup, or hybrid) are available and economically feasible.
Operator latency tolerance is ≤500ms (typical for heavy equipment teleoperation); FEC and redundancy must not exceed this budget.
Safety Services module is available to receive SafetyAlert messages and trigger operator warnings or mission abort.
Vehicle module provides telemetry at a known rate (e.g., 10 Hz) and can accept commands at a known rate (e.g., 50 Hz).
open_risks: RISK: FEC adds latency and computational overhead. If latency budget is tight, FEC may degrade operator responsiveness.
RISK: Automatic failover logic must be robust; incorrect failover decisions could cause command loss or duplication.
RISK: Priority queuing may starve non-critical telemetry during congestion, reducing operator situational awareness.
RISK: Network conditions may vary significantly across worksites, making a one-size-fits-all packet loss target infeasible.
RISK: Redundant channels may not be available in remote or underground worksites, limiting the effectiveness of the mitigation strategy.
evidence_needed: Network stress test results under agreed worksite conditions (bandwidth, latency, interference profiles) demonstrating ≤1% packet loss with DP-D implemented.
Latency measurements for FEC encoding/decoding and failover logic to confirm operator responsiveness is not degraded.
Failover test results demonstrating correct behavior under primary channel loss, secondary channel loss, and simultaneous loss scenarios.
Priority queue test results showing that safety-critical messages are protected while non-critical telemetry is deferred during congestion.
Operator acceptance testing confirming that teleoperation is safe and effective with the implemented packet loss mitigation.
